food_subgroup: Noodle dish with meat — udon with chicken sausage in curry broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general udon noodle dish class (FOODON_00002802); no specific code exists for chicken sausage curry udon. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers cooked udon noodles as the dominant carbohydrate component; no composite curry-udon dish code exists. Closest parent used for the noodle base.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for chicken sausage curry udon or a sufficiently close parent dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken sausage curry udon, approximately 450 g, comprising thick udon noodles, sliced chicken sausage, and a rich Japanese curry broth.

nutrient_source.title: Curry udon (Japanese style) — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Udon noodles cooked (FDC ID 168917), Chicken sausage (FDC ID 172949), and Japanese curry roux (FDC ID 174276); proportions estimated at 50% noodles, 20% chicken sausage, 30% curry broth/roux by weight.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for chicken sausage curry udon exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked udon noodles (~50% by weight), chicken sausage (~20%), and Japanese curry broth/roux (~30%). Weighted composite calculation applied. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Udon noodles, white, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to chicken sausage curry udon dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 55 for boiled white udon noodles (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Chicken sausage and curry broth components have negligible independent GI contribution relative to the noodle base. The dish GI is estimated at 55 (low) based on the udon noodle dominant carbohydrate fraction. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 43.3 g, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 17.5 × 4.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~2160 mg per 450 g serving) from curry broth and processed chicken sausage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load per serving, which can exceed 90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~43 per serving) driven by the large udon noodle carbohydrate portion despite a low GI of 55
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of the high total glycemic load per standard bowl serving; reducing portion size to approximately 200–250 g or substituting with a smaller noodle portion can help manage postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken sausage and curry roux
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content and saturated fat from processed chicken sausage present a moderate cardiovascular risk; choosing a lower-sodium broth and limiting sausage quantity is recommended to reduce overall cardiovascular burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Sausage Curry Udon is a Japanese noodle dish featuring thick, chewy udon noodles served in a rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry broth, topped with sliced chicken sausage. The dish is a fusion of the classic Japanese curry flavour profile — characterised by a smooth, slightly sweet and savoury roux-based sauce — with the hearty texture of udon noodles and the mild smokiness of chicken sausage. It is typically prepared by simmering dashi-based stock with Japanese curry roux, boiling udon noodles separately, and combining them in a bowl with sliced pre-cooked chicken sausage and garnishes such as spring onion. The dish is popular in Japanese casual dining and ramen-style restaurants, including in Singapore where it appears on menus such as GyoGyo at Funan. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~118 kcal per 100 g) with a significant carbohydrate contribution from udon noodles and a notable sodium load from the curry broth and processed sausage (~480 mg sodium per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 450 g serving is high (~43 g), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to wheat-based udon noodles and curry roux. It contains moderate protein from chicken sausage and is a source of B vitamins and iron.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for chicken sausage curry udon was found in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked udon noodles, chicken sausage, and Japanese curry broth using weighted proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white udon noodles. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component foods per USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on approximate dish composition: 50% cooked udon noodles, 20% chicken sausage, 30% Japanese curry broth/roux by weight. The restaurant source (GyoGyo - Funan, field survey data) is preserved as provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. GI estimated at 55 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white udon noodles; gi_category is low per the Veritas threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). Glycemic load per 450 g serving ≈ 43.3 g. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record; however, this dish is a composite noodle dish and could also be classified under 'Mixed'; the input classification is retained as the dominant protein component is chicken sausage.
